items.title: 'Why get up if you have nowhere to go?': NDIS participants slam cuts

children.content: NDIS participants set up their lives around the scheme's rules. But now the rules are changing and they fear what lies ahead.

articles.title: Regional families and carers fear they'll bear the brunt of NDIS cuts

children.content: People living with disability in regional areas already face extra hurdles in accessing support and families fear changes to the NDIS could have irreversible consequences.

image.altText: A young boy and girl both wearing glasses

articles.title: How the new eligibility test for NDIS participants could work

children.content: Labor's plan to create a new "assessment tool" to determine future NDIS eligibility based on functional need could backfire without the right settings, say experts.

image.altText: A close up of a man's hands in his lap.

gallery.altText: A white gender-diverse young adult with long red hair sitting in a wheelchair at a kitchen table
A white adult man with glasses and a black brimmed hat sitting in a wheelchair

thumbnail.altText: A white gender-diverse young adult with long red hair sitting in a wheelchair at a kitchen table
A white adult man with glasses and a black brimmed hat sitting in a wheelchair

items.title: Plan to end NDIS 'wild west' will flip rules on their head for providers

children.content: NDIS Minister Mark Butler will end the practice of handwritten invoices, providers without qualifications and a "let it rip" market of profiteering operators. But the sector warns changes must be navigated carefully.

image.altText: An NDIS logo is seen on a building

items.title: Politically, baby boomers' day has come

children.content: The Albanese government is reading the room when it comes to intergenerational equity ahead of the May 12 budget.

image.altText: A man in suit and tie holds his hands up in front of him as he speaks seriously.

images.altText: donald trump points his finger at a colleague, he is sat down and dressed in a navy suit
Smoke rises from a village in southern Lebanon as the Israeli army operates tanks in the area

articles.title: If ships are being fired upon and seized how can there be a ceasefire?

children.content: As part of its blockade of Iran, ships have been seized by US forces, others fired upon by Iran and, most recently, boarded by the regime's revolutionary guards. This is all while both sides maintain there is a ceasefire.

image.altText: Masked men in sand-coloured army fatigues climbing a ladder off the side of a ship.

articles.title: Indonesian minister suggests tax on another key shipping route

children.content: The suggestion of levying ships using the Strait of Malacca was suggested and then walked back by Indonesia's finance minister, but has prompted a response from Canberra.

image.altText: Dozens of cargo vessels stationed in a water way

children.content: Donald Trump says the US will work with Lebanon to "protect itself from Hezbollah" and that he will meet with Benjamin Netanyahu soon. Follow live.

items.title: Trump says ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel extended for three weeks

updates.title: Lebanese and Israeli ambassadors thank Trump for 'historic' moment
Ceasefire between Lebanon and Israel extended for three weeks, Trump says
IDF: Missile launches from Lebanon into northern Israel intercepted

items.title: What happened when I messaged the number threatening slain reporter

children.content: Talks in Washington are set against a backdrop of decades-long disputes and deep mistrust, but at least they're happening.

image.altText: Man with Jerusalem in the background

items.title: Australia must 'get serious' about defence to be better ally to US, Hastie says

children.content: Liberal frontbencher Andrew Hastie uses a speech ahead of Anzac Day to warn Australia must bring more to its alliance with the US, and appreciate that it is a relationship built on strategic value, not sentimentality. Follow live.

image.altText: Andrew Hastie press gallery doorstop

items.title: 'Finfluencers' suspected of giving unlawful advice hit with warning notices

children.content: The warning notice to the so-called "finfluencers" relates to suspected provision of unlicensed financial advice, including promoting claims of guaranteed returns.

image.altText: hands holding a phone as the stock market runs behind it

items.title: 'This is now terminal': UK braces for prospect of sixth PM in seven years

children.content: Sir Keir Starmer, who led Labour to a landslide general election victory in July 2024, has become so unpopular that even some colleagues are framing his demise as a case of not if, but when.

image.altText: A man in a suit looking down

items.title: How Aussies near you are coping with the fuel crisis

children.content: Petrol prices are reshaping Australian life in ways big and small — from a grandmother's isolation to a farmer's future.

articles.title: Warnings of power price rises, missed renewable targets under proposed tax

children.content: The treasurer says changes to capital gains tax for foreign investors is a clarification, but experts say it could dampen investment.

image.altText: Wind turbines among clouds.

articles.title: Fuel filter: What to expect on Australian fuel supply and pricing for the weekend

children.content: The week's most important fuel news, analysis and pricing information.

image.altText: Fuel gauge graphic with dollar sign

articles.title: Viva Energy boss says more refineries needed for secure fuel supply

children.content: The Viva Energy refinery provides about 10 per cent of Australia's fuel. Its chief, Scott Wyatt, chats to Alan Kohler about the company's future in the wake of the Geelong fire.

image.altText: An oil refinery

gallery.altText: Three people who are affected by high fuel prices.

thumbnail.altText: Three people who are affected by high fuel prices.

items.title: Oil prices spike again just as Australian fuel prices drop

children.content: The price of unleaded petrol is back to just 10 cents a litre more than before the war, even as oil prices are spiking again on global markets. Follow the day's events and insights as they happen with our business reporters on the ABC News live markets blog.

image.altText: ASX

items.title: The legacy of once-banned soldiers that hides in plain sight

children.content: Thousands of Indian soldiers served alongside Anzac forces at Gallipoli, leaving their legacy in the "puggaree" — a traditional Indian headwrap that was adapted to adorn the slouch hats worn by the Australian army.

image.altText: Six men with red and white striped turbans in khaki uniforms with red and white around their waist and long white socks.

items.title: Ancient fossils reveal when the iconic Twelve Apostles formed

children.content: An analysis of tiny fossils of sea creatures found in rock samples around the Port Campbell region in south-western Victoria has refined our understanding of how the Twelve Apostles formed.

gallery.altText: Four limestone monoliths of varying size lapped at by shoreline waves next to massive cliffs.

items.title: 'They deserve death': Officer used internal system to monitor police wife

children.content: Text messages read in court set out how a Tasmania Police officer monitored his wife's movements, including via an internal dispatching system, as part of a 15-month family violence matter.

image.altText: A text message that says: "Those people on your shift are fucking dead."

items.title: US watchdog announces Epstein files audit

children.content: The audit follows months of controversy over censorship, missed deadlines and the publication of survivors' details.

image.altText: ghislaine maxwell and jeffrey epstein seen together on a black background

items.title: Jane Fonda was in her 40s when she changed the way we exercise

children.content: In the 1980s, the fitness industry as we know it was just getting started. One of the most important people in developing the way we exercise today was Jane Fonda, who released a best-selling aerobics video in 1982.

gallery.altText: Actress Jane Fonda in a yellow exercise outfit stretching with her left foot pointed upward in the air.

thumbnail.altText: Actress Jane Fonda in a yellow exercise outfit stretching with her left foot pointed upward in the air.

items.title: Figure skating teen makes history for Australia

children.content: At just 15, Hana Bath became the first Australian female to ever land a triple axel in competition. The rising star now has her eye on the 2030 Winter Olympics.

image.altText: A figure skater wearing skates on ice holders her hands in the air

items.title: Weekly news quiz: Trump's eleventh-hour announcement, a disabled bird and a speedy humanoid robot

children.content: It's Friday, and that means it's time to test your news knowledge from the past seven days. Were you paying attention this week?

image.altText: A red and black robot on a street with barriers in the background and a ribbon falling to the ground.

items.title: Sheraz risked his life translating in Afghanistan. His family paid the price

children.content: Sheraz worked with Australian and US troops in Afghanistan, as a result his family were targeted by the Taliban. This Anzac Day he's being heroed.

gallery.altText: A man seated at a table, smiling.

thumbnail.altText: A man seated at a table, smiling.

items.title: Which states get a public holiday for Anzac Day?

children.content: Anzac Day is observed on April 25 every year. This year that date falls on a Saturday, but that doesn't mean a long weekend for everyone.

image.altText: poppies on a cenotaph

items.title: 'Genuinely speechless': Appeal board's stunning claim in homophobia case

children.content: The appeal board in the Lance Collard homophobia case says homophobic and racist language is commonplace in the AFL due to the "highly competitive" nature of the game.

image.altText: Lance Collard stands with both arms in the air during a St Kilda vs West Coast Eagles AFL match in 2025.

items.title: Hackers steal $3.7m from Sri Lanka earmarked for debts to Australia

children.content: Cyber criminals have hacked Sri Lanka's finance ministry, stealing millions of dollars destined to repay Australia.

image.altText: Cyberattack illustration

items.title: Charts explain why fertiliser prices aren't coming down anytime soon

children.content: Farmers around the world are going to be paying more for one of their most important inputs, fertiliser. Here are four charts that explain why.

image.altText: A hand holds brilliant white fertiliser while a truck tips it off in the background

items.title: Hundreds of fishing boats sit idle off southern China — but they're not there to fish

children.content: Off China's southern coast, hundreds of fishing boats sit idle. The crews aren't there to fish, but rather to "maintain presence" as part of a shadowy, state-funded fleet.

gallery.altText: A satellite image giving a birdseye view of boats scattered across a bay.

thumbnail.altText: A satellite image giving a birdseye view of boats scattered across a bay.

items.title: Adelaide Festival appoints new Writers' Week director in wake of crisis

children.content: The new director of Adelaide Writers' Week says she is hopeful "really important lessons have been learned" following the implosion of this year's literary festival.

image.altText: A smiling woman leans an arm over a park bench

items.title: Bulldogs offer update after star's horror fall as Curnow kicks a bag

children.content: Sydney's hold on top spot is strengthened by a 66-point win over the Western Bulldogs, whose injury woes continue.

image.altText: nauigthon

items.title: Marshall fumes at NRL refs as Koroisau faces hip-drop tackle verdict

children.content: Wests Tigers coach Benji Marshall has launched a passionate criticism of NRL officiating, following his side's 33-14 win over the Canberra Raiders.

image.altText: NRL coach in cap and hooded top, looking on during a match, stern look

items.title: After tornado warnings and midnight matches, Wallaroos are ready for NZ

children.content: Having survived the threat of tornadoes during their US tour, the Wallaroos are enjoying a distraction-free build-up to their Anzac Day Test against New Zealand.

image.altText: Wallaroos and Canadian players during a Test in the United States.

items.title: 'Sad for us': Hannah Clarke's parents step back from advocacy

children.content: After their daughter and three grandchildren were murdered, Sue and Lloyd Clarke began a charity to improve awareness of coercive control. Now they've decided they need to step back for their own health.

thumbnail.altText: Hannah Clarke, hugs her son Trey Baxter, while standing on a beach.

items.title: Now James Valentine is gone, I'll let you in on the secret of his success

children.content: Children grew up with him, adults grew old with him, he kept us connected to our city, the world and each other. James helped us make sense of life and gave us permission to laugh at the absurdity of it all — and ourselves.

image.altText: James Valentine, wearing a read jumper and black headphones, smiling and talking into a microphone.

items.title: Documents reveal fresh details about Roberts-Smith's overseas moving plans

children.content: Police were concerned war veteran Ben Roberts-Smith was attempting to move overseas and possibly to Singapore or Spain, court documents reveal.

image.altText: Ben Roberts-Smith looks at the camera outside a court building

items.title: Historic hotel of celebrities, royalty and lovers of the paranormal for sale

children.content: From hosting royalty to stars popping in for a steak, the hotel has a spooky past with tales of a jilted lover.

items.title: Young stroke survivor works to raise awareness as NT falls behind

children.content: When Alison Reidy, then 38, woke in terrible pain, she had no idea she was having a stroke. Now she's trying to improve stroke awareness in the Northern Territory.
